claims.claim: The Volcani Institute / Agricultural Research Organization is Israel's national agricultural research body, hosting Kc (crop coefficient) tables and Israeli-context agronomy research at agri.gov.il.
Israel's Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Development publishes subsidy programs, regulations, and certifications at gov.il/en/departments/ministry_of_agriculture_and_rural_development.
Israel's Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Development Hebrew homepage is at gov.il/he/departments/ministry_of_agriculture_and_rural_development.
Israel's Water Authority publishes water allocation quotas, agricultural-tariff updates, and reclaimed-water rules at gov.il/en/departments/water_authority.
Israel's Water Authority Hebrew homepage is at gov.il/he/departments/water_authority.
The Israel Innovation Authority publishes agritech grants and pilot funding programs at innovationisrael.org.il/en.
The Israel Innovation Authority Hebrew homepage is at innovationisrael.org.il.
Start-Up Nation Central publishes an Israeli AgriFoodTech industry directory tracking approximately 600-750 Israeli agritech companies and their funding stages.

Netafim GrowSphere is a consumer-facing irrigation product without a documented public API; the URL growsphere.netafim.com/api/v1 used in SKILL examples is labeled illustrative-only, and integrators must contact Netafim directly for partnership API access.
CropX's published REST API root is documented at api.cropx.com/v2 (illustrative usage in SKILL examples).
Taranis's published REST API root is documented at api.taranis.com/v1 (illustrative usage in SKILL examples).
The Israel Meteorological Service MCP (ims-mcp) is listed in the skills-il MCP directory under government-services and provides rain, ET0, and station data.
Phytech, an Israeli plant-based water-stress-sensor company, acquired Saturas (the stem-water-potential sensor company) in 2023.
Israel recycles approximately 85% or more of its treated wastewater for agricultural reuse, the highest rate of any country.
Drip irrigation (invented in Israel at Kibbutz Hatzerim by Netafim, 1965) achieves field efficiencies of approximately 90-95% in modern Israeli installations.
Published precision-agriculture case studies routinely report water savings in the range of 20-40% versus uniform irrigation when sensor-driven scheduling and crop coefficients are used.
Management Allowable Depletion (MAD) is a standard irrigation scheduling parameter; values around 50% are commonly used for moderately drought-sensitive crops.
Quota-tracking systems for agricultural water typically alert at 80% and 95% of allocation; the specific thresholds are operator-chosen, not regulatory.
